Guideline: Use typed extractors for request parsing
Description: Axum extractors automatically parse path parameters, query strings, JSON bodies, and headers with type safety
Do: Use Path<T>, Query<T>, Json<T>, Extension<T> extractors; derive Deserialize for types
Dont: Manually parse request body or query strings; use raw string matching for path params

Description: Group related routes under a common prefix using Router::nest for clean API organization
Do: Use nest for API versioning and resource grouping; merge routers from different modules
Dont: Define all routes in a single flat router; duplicate path prefixes

Guideline: Share application state with State extractor
Description: Use State<T> to share application state (DB pool, config) across handlers. State is Arc-wrapped internally
Do: Put shared state in a struct; use State<AppState> extractor; derive Clone for the state type
Dont: Use global statics for state; clone pools in every handler; pass state through extension layers

Description: Create a custom error type that implements IntoResponse to return structured JSON errors with proper HTTP status codes
Do: Map domain errors to HTTP status codes; log internal errors; never expose internal details to clients
Dont: Return plain text errors; expose database error messages; use StatusCode alone without body

Description: Axum is built on Tower; use tower::ServiceBuilder and tower-http for standard middleware
Do: Chain layers with ServiceBuilder; apply middleware to specific route groups; use tower-http for CORS, compression, tracing
Dont: Write middleware from scratch for common needs; apply all middleware globally when some should be route-specific

Description: Create custom middleware functions that have access to request, next, and state
Do: Use from_fn for simple middleware; use from_fn_with_state for state-dependent middleware; call next.run(req).await
Dont: Block inside middleware; forget to call next.run(); modify response body without updating content-length

Description: Use validator crate with a custom ValidatedJson extractor to automatically validate incoming data
Do: Create a wrapper extractor that deserializes and validates; return 400 with validation errors
Dont: Accept any input without validation; validate in the handler body instead of at the extractor level

Description: Use axum's built-in WebSocket support for real-time communication
Do: Use ws::WebSocketUpgrade for upgrading; handle ping/pong; implement graceful shutdown for WS connections
Dont: Keep WebSocket connections alive indefinitely without heartbeat; block the handler

Description: Use axum's test utilities to send mock requests and verify responses without starting a server
Do: Use TestClient or tower::ServiceExt for testing; test both success and error paths; verify status codes and response bodies
Dont: Start a real HTTP server for unit tests; only test happy paths

Description: Use axum's streaming body support for large responses like file downloads or Server-Sent Events
Do: Use StreamBody or Sse for streaming; set appropriate Content-Type; handle backpressure
Dont: Buffer entire response in memory before sending; use Vec<u8> for large files

Description: Use tower-http CorsLayer to configure Cross-Origin Resource Sharing for API access from browsers
Do: Configure allowed origins, methods, and headers explicitly; use CorsLayer::permissive() only in development
Dont: Allow all origins in production; forget to include required headers; set overly long max_age
Code Bad: let cors = CorsLayer::permissive();\n// allows ALL origins, ALL methods, ALL headers\n// insecure in production

Description: Listen for termination signals and drain in-flight requests before shutting down
Do: Use tokio::signal for SIGTERM/SIGINT; use axum::serve with .with_graceful_shutdown(); drain connections
Dont: Call process::exit() directly; ignore shutdown signals in containers

Description: Extract and verify JWT tokens in middleware; inject authenticated user into request extensions
Do: Verify token signature and expiration; extract claims into a typed struct; use Extension for downstream access
Dont: Store secrets in code; skip token expiration checks; return detailed auth errors to unauthorized users

Description: Use tower-governor or custom middleware to limit request rates per client IP or API key
Do: Configure per-route or per-group rate limits; use sliding window or token bucket; respond with 429 and Retry-After header
Dont: Allow unlimited requests; rate limit globally instead of per-client

Description: Use tower-http TraceLayer for automatic request/response tracing with spans and timing
Do: Configure TraceLayer with custom span names; include request ID; use tracing-subscriber for output formatting
Dont: Use println! for request logging; not log response times; miss error logging

Description: Use tower-http ServeDir for serving static files with proper caching headers
Do: Configure ServeDir with fallback; set cache headers; use compression for text assets
Dont: Implement custom file serving logic; serve files without cache headers

Description: Use axum::extract::Multipart for file upload processing with size limits
Do: Set size limits; validate file types; stream to disk for large files; use DefaultBodyLimit layer
Dont: Load entire file into memory; skip content type validation; allow unlimited upload sizes

Description: Provide /health (liveness) and /ready (readiness) endpoints for container orchestrators
Do: Check database connectivity in /ready; keep /health lightweight; add version info
Dont: Return 200 always regardless of system state; include sensitive info in health response

Guideline: Version APIs with path or header
Description: Use path-based (/api/v1/) or header-based versioning to manage API evolution
Do: Use Router::nest for path-based versioning; maintain backward compatibility; use Accept header for content negotiation
Dont: Change existing endpoints without versioning; mix versioning strategies
Code Bad: let app = Router::new()\n    .route("/api/users", get(list_users));\n// no versioning: any change breaks existing clients

Description: Use utoipa to generate OpenAPI/Swagger docs from Rust types and handlers
Do: Derive ToSchema on types; annotate handlers with #[utoipa::path]; serve Swagger UI
Dont: Maintain OpenAPI specs manually; let docs drift from implementation
Code Bad: // Manually maintained openapi.yaml that is out of date\n// Endpoints exist in code but not in docs\n// Docs describe endpoints that no longer exist

Description: Wrap related database operations in a transaction to ensure atomicity
Do: Use sqlx::PgPool::begin() for transactions; commit explicitly; handle rollback on error
Dont: Execute related queries without a transaction; assume partial success is acceptable

Description: Use cursor-based or keyset pagination for consistent results with large datasets
Do: Use a cursor (last_id) instead of offset for large tables; limit page size; return next cursor in response
Dont: Use OFFSET pagination for large tables (slow); allow unlimited page sizes

Guideline: Use proper response types and status codes
Description: Return appropriate HTTP status codes with typed response bodies
Do: Use StatusCode for empty responses; Json<T> for JSON; (StatusCode, Json<T>) for custom status + body
Dont: Always return 200; return plain strings instead of JSON; inconsistent response shapes

Description: Axum requires body-consuming extractors (Json, Multipart) to be last in handler arguments
Do: Place State, Path, Query before Json; only one body extractor per handler
Dont: Put Json before Path; use multiple body extractors

Description: Set appropriate pool sizes based on workload and database limits
Do: Set min and max connections; configure idle timeout; match pool size to Tokio worker threads
Dont: Use default pool settings without tuning; set max connections higher than database allows
Code Good:
let pool = PgPoolOptions::new()
    .max_connections(20)
    .min_connections(5)
    .acquire_timeout(std::time::Duration::from_secs(3))
    .idle_timeout(std::time::Duration::from_secs(600))
    .connect(&database_url)
    .await?;
Code Bad: let pool = PgPool::connect(&database_url).await?;\n// default pool: 10 connections, no idle timeout\n// may be too many or too few for your workload

Description: Generate a unique request ID for each request and include it in all log entries for correlation
Do: Use tower-http RequestIdLayer or generate UUID in middleware; include in response headers; use tracing spans
Dont: Not correlating log entries across a request lifecycle; using random log messages without context

Description: Use tower-http CompressionLayer to compress responses automatically based on Accept-Encoding header
Do: Enable compression for API responses; exclude already-compressed formats (images, videos)
Dont: Send uncompressed JSON responses; compress already-compressed content

Description: Use tower-http TimeoutLayer to prevent slow requests from holding connections indefinitely
Do: Set timeouts per route or globally; use tower::timeout for fine-grained control; return 408 on timeout
Dont: Allow requests to run indefinitely; set the same timeout for all routes regardless of expected duration

Description: Use tower's catch_panic or axum's HandleErrorLayer to convert panics and service errors into proper responses
Do: Catch panics in production; log the backtrace; return 500 with a safe message
Dont: Let panics crash the server; return raw error details

Description: Use environment variables following 12-factor app principles with typed deserialization
Do: Use dotenvy for .env files in development; deserialize into typed Config struct; validate at startup
Dont: Hardcode configuration; use unwrap on env vars; mix config sources inconsistently
Code Bad: let port: u16 = std::env::var("PORT").unwrap().parse().unwrap();\nlet db_url = std::env::var("DATABASE_URL").unwrap();\n// panics on missing vars; no defaults; no validation
